LinkedIn and 3rd parties use essential and non-essential cookies to provide, secure, analyze and improve our Services, and to show you relevant ads (including professional and job ads) on and off LinkedIn. Learn more in our Cookie Policy.

Select Accept to consent or Reject to decline non-essential cookies for this use. You can update your choices at any time in your settings.

Why mass applying to jobs won't get you hired

This title was summarized by AI from the post below.
Kyle Winterbottom
1y
  • Report this post

I saw a post on here that had gone viral It showed you how to find jobs that had just been posted within the hour Stating that being one of the first to apply to a job gave you more chance of securing an interview An insightful post and hard to argue with the logic However, the post ended with a link that was created to show people how to set up an auto-fill for applications Aka; 'Easy Apply' on steroids The amount of irrelevance is an at an all time high We've stopped advertising because to be frank it's a waste of time Finding jobs most recently posted is a great thing Mass applying to everything within a certain market isn't It's an employers market out there in the D&A world And they're being as specific as they've ever been Specificity is key Mass applying using easy apply and auto fill is a living contradiction to what organisations want If you're serious about getting a new job Hand select the jobs you are qualified to apply for Apply, reach out directly, email, LinkedIn message, call, find common connections and ask for a referral, etc Go above and beyond That's much more likely to get a positive outcome than mass applying to lots of jobs In this market, less is more #data #analytics #jobs

44 31 Comments
Like Comment
Vamshi Malyala™ 1y
  • Report this comment

Biggest unspoken truth in hiring Data Leaders: It’s not about what they’ve done— It’s about where, how, and in what madness they did it. Hiring without context is like drafting a Formula 1 driver to race a tractor. Sure, they’re both vehicles—but good luck getting pole position at Silverstone. I’m deep in the trenches building out a D&A hiring desk—and this post? It’s the blueprint for what I’m trying to preach every damn day. Which brings me to this: A collab between us wouldn’t be just another content piece. It would be like Iron Man and Doctor Strange drawing up a hiring spellbook—with sass, science, and maybe a little chaos. 🌀⚡ Let’s give the industry something it didn’t know it needed. The kind of thing that makes hiring managers nod, wince, and whisper: “Damn. They’re right.” You in for it?

Like
Reply
1 Reaction
Dan Easterbrook 1y
  • Report this comment

Easy Apply is a curse! Auto-fill even worse. Agree 100%. Target your search and seek out mutual connections, direct contacts etc. I understand there must be a pressure to secure a role if out of work but the number of people that post saying that they have applied for 100s of jobs, how targeted are they? There aren't hundreds of data leader jobs for example, so don't apply recklessly. Quality over quantity!

Like
Reply
2 Reactions 3 Reactions
Lucy Honeyman 1y
  • Report this comment

Couldn’t agree more Kyle Winterbottom! Quick apply = Quick waste of time! I’d advise anyone to spend more time on fewer applications. Kyle is spot on, data roles have been very very few and far between for a long time! But don’t let that hold you back!! Send your CV speculatively to the organisations you like and have a great cover note so they remember you as soon as anything becomes available. Have a caring Recruiter (like myself 👋) on your side, especially if they specialise in your area - I can coach you and help get on the radar with the managers who will eventually need someone. It’s a time to be strategic and show you can adapt and think outside the conventional box! THAT will get you hired. Mindless quick applies will not. Good luck all!

Like
Reply
1 Reaction
Sarah Nell-Rodriquez 1y
  • Report this comment

I really appreciate this post and the intention behind it—quality over quantity definitely matters. But I also want to gently call out what I’m seeing, especially here in the U.S. I know so many people right now who are doing everything right: they’re focused, specific, going above and beyond—and still, they’re without a job. At some point, it becomes less about the perfect application and more about the humanity in the process. These folks don’t just need advice—they need empathy, advocacy, and a little generosity from the people who do have access and opportunity. If candidates are showing up with everything they’ve got, the least we can do is meet them there.

Like
Reply
1 Reaction 2 Reactions
Edward Chenard 1y
  • Report this comment

I just saw a post from another recruiter, specifically talking about the executive job market, giving the advice that it is a numbers game and to just hit everyone up. Basically the exact opposite of what you are saying. This is why that post gets traction, the message candidates here is completely conflicted. A desperate person will try anything and this market is telling people to basically do anything.

Like
Reply
1 Reaction 2 Reactions
Dan Blake 1y
  • Report this comment

At the lower grades it’s a numbers game - and I get why people do this as they need to get experience and secure an interview. Candidates in work or are selective will not be the first applicants- they need time for them to complete and do due diligence on the company and opportunity. Less is more dependant on the amount of experience you have.

More Relevant Posts

  • Omer idrees
    9mo
    • Report this post

    It happened again. Another jobseeker went from ignored to in-demand in less than a month. As usual, they followed these 3 steps: 1-Clarified their unique edge. 2-Built a social-proof portfolio that went beyond the resume 3-Positioned themselves with authority content that showed how they think, solve problems, and deliver After helping 500+ jobseekers, it’s proven: recruiters don’t just hire resumes they hire clarity, credibility, and confidence. And here’s the big shift most people miss… A resume is passive. A portfolio plus authority content gives people an experience of you your voice, your thinking, your promise. That’s what builds trust. That’s what sparks referrals. That’s what turns “just another applicant” into “the candidate we need to meet.” This is why one visibility system (clarity + proof + positioning) beats spraying applications across job boards, endlessly editing CVs, or relying on AI shortcuts. Slowing down is speeding up. Let everyone else stay invisible. You? Double down on showing your promise. Please… Stop hoping to be discovered. Start becoming strategically discoverable.

    Like Comment

    To view or add a comment, sign in

  • Todd Minter
    9mo
    • Report this post

    I recently spoke with a client who has a position that has been open for 6 months. The job ad looked fine, so what was the problem, he asked? My advice: 1: If you have a lot of applicants, the problem may be in your screening. Often, recruiters will eliminate candidates they feel are 'overqualified', or (especially with AI screening tools) perhaps have good experience but it's not the 'right' experience. Maybe it's time to talk to some of those candidates and see if they are a good fit after all. 2: Turn off the feature on your ATS that prevents candidates from applying multiple times to the same job. At worst, you have to read some bad resumes a second time. At best, their qualifications seem better on a second review. 3: Make sure your listed salary is reasonable relative to the qualifications. A friend in the restaurant industry was advertising minimum wage, but requiring a minimum of 3 years of experience. Why would an experienced person bother applying to a minimum wage job? Or a person with ten years of engineering experience apply to a position offering entry level pay? 4: Mix up where you are advertising. If you are only using LinkedIn, you are missing out on candidates who don't use it for job hunting. Insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting a different result. Don't be insane, be creative and try something different.

    8
    Like Comment

    To view or add a comment, sign in

  • Maryanne Miller, MHA
    8mo
    • Report this post

    🚨 The 2025 Job Search: What’s Real, What’s Not? It feels like every job seeker I talk to says the same thing: 👉 “I’ve applied to hundreds of jobs… but I’m not sure half of them are even real.” And honestly? They might be right. The job application process in 2025 has become a maze. Between mass postings, recycled listings, “evergreen” roles that never seem to close, and AI-generated job ads, it’s harder than ever to tell what’s legitimate. Some trends we’re all seeing: 💼 Hundreds (or thousands) of applicants per posting. Even for mid-level roles. 🤖 AI-driven job listings—some created for data scraping or engagement, not real hiring. 🔁 Old jobs reposted every few weeks just to “look active.” 🚩 Fake recruiter outreach on LinkedIn or email, often with convincing logos and copy. It’s exhausting. And it’s making talented people question their confidence, their resumes, and sometimes their worth. So how do you tell what’s real? * Check the company’s site — is the job listed there, or only on aggregators? * Look for human engagement — real recruiters, LinkedIn profiles with activity, or employee posts about the role. * Trust your gut — if the posting sounds too broad, too perfect, or oddly generic, it probably is. * Network where possible — a referral or a DM to a real team member still cuts through the noise faster than any “Apply Now” button. It’s not just about applying anymore — it’s about filtering through the noise to find authentic opportunities. If you’ve been navigating this, I’d love to hear: 👉 How are you spotting real versus fake opportunities in 2025? 👉 Have you noticed an uptick in “ghost listings”?

    11
    Like Comment

    To view or add a comment, sign in

  • Charlotte Davies
    8mo
    • Report this post

    The job market is tougher than ever and standing out has never mattered more. I spoke to the MailOnline about how job seekers can rise above the noise as AI and automation reshape the hiring process. As I shared: “Standing out to recruiters right now isn't about gimmicks - it's about clarity, relevance, and confidence in what you bring to the table as a professional.” Here are three quick ways to strengthen your applications 👇 💡 Keep it clear: Overly designed CVs can confuse automated systems. Clarity always beats cleverness. 💡 Show your skills: Focus on what you can do, not just job titles - highlight results and impact. 💡 Stay discoverable: Keep your LinkedIn profile current and keyword-rich so recruiters can find you. https://lnkd.in/eNf6PUTs

    How to make sure your job application beats AI and doesn't get lost dailymail.co.uk
    31 1 Comment
    Like Comment

    To view or add a comment, sign in

  • Paul McAlpine
    9mo
    • Report this post

    Why can't people get a job anymore? So much to unpack here - and to be fair - the article does cover it well. Strap in. But this does show what both potential employees and employers are up against. Auto-apply, AI, cutbacks, economic uncertainty, rising employment costs, survival modes, you name it. Applying shouldn't be hard, but also, it shouldn't be one click. Taking the time to really think if you can do the job, if you're qualified for the job or if you even want the job can prevent frustration on both sides. AI - is getting better, it's by no means the finished article. If you're out of work and feel your 'competing' with AI for a role, I suggest learning how to use it to be better in your role and market yourself with that. It will cause displacement, so either get ahead or get left behind. 30% drop in grad roles according to Adzuna - not a shock really. Considering everything else that's going on, competition, AI, cost of hiring and having qualified experienced hires on the market has made this an unbelievably tough time to be entering the workforce as a graduate. The rise of the employer NI costs, painful. This has forced companies not only to look at every role and make sure they have value, it's also had companies look at whether the roles they need to hire for are really essential - oftentimes they are - but what this has done is make companies far more cautious with hiring, which makes hiring processes longer and more frustrating for candidates. Survival mode - it's pretty much all of us now. Thanks for coming to my TED Talk. What's your take on the hiring market at the moment? #Hiring #Jobs #Recruitment #Engineering https://lnkd.in/erSe6c7B

    Why people can’t get a job any more msn.com
    5
    Like Comment

    To view or add a comment, sign in

  • Eduardo Ramirez
    9mo
    • Report this post

    There has been a noticeable trend of posts criticizing individuals for not perfecting their job applications, emphasizing the importance of demonstrating effort to impress potential employers. This raises two important discussions: First, it's essential to submit a high-quality application. I agree that a lack of intent and effort can be frustrating for companies reviewing applications. Second, in the current job market, quality often does not outweigh quantity. If it did, many of us would not be in the position of searching for jobs, as we would already be employed. It is easier to dedicate time to crafting the perfect application when one has a job to support them. Expecting that only well-tailored applications come from the best candidates, while overlooking the realities of the job market, can be both insulting and naive. The current job market excludes many individuals who are desperate for employment. Those in need often cannot afford to submit only a few highly tailored applications; they must send out as many as possible. If we want a job market that prioritizes quality, we need to foster an environment that is inclusive, not reliant on AI software that can filter out qualified candidates due to minor formatting issues, and that genuinely values and rewards quality over quantity.

    3
    Like Comment

    To view or add a comment, sign in

  • Adam Gonnerman
    8mo
    • Report this post

    The job search has its share of frustrations — especially in today’s market. Here are a few that really stand out for me: 🔹 Ghosting – Recruiters or hiring managers go silent after initial conversations or interviews. 🔹 Spam “recruiters” – Messages about roles that are miles away or totally unrelated to my experience. 🔹 MLM pitches – People disguising multi-level marketing schemes as “business opportunities.” 🔹 Over-specific job descriptions – Listings demanding a mix of skills that no single human could possibly have. 🔹 AI “virtual recruiters” – Automated calls that feel impersonal and frustrating. 🔹 Repetitive application forms – Upload a résumé, then re-enter all the same information manually. 🔹 LinkedIn job feed clutter – Jobs I’ve already applied to keep showing up again and again. 🔹 Perpetually reposted jobs – Same listings appearing month after month with no real intent to hire. 🔹Resume rewrite offers - People offering to rewrite my resume, for a fee. Despite it all, I’m still out here applying every day — because that’s the only way forward. #JobSearch #OpenToWork

    12 1 Comment
    Like Comment

    To view or add a comment, sign in

  • Dr Patrice Green
    8mo
    • Report this post

    The job search has changed—dramatically. What once worked five years ago no longer guarantees results today. Job boards are flooded with applicants, artificial intelligence filters hundreds of résumés before a human ever looks at them, and employers are now hiring for alignment as much as ability. In this new landscape, success is not about how many applications you send—it’s about the strategy behind each one. Yet many professionals still approach their job search like a numbers game, hoping that volume will eventually lead to victory. The truth is, it won’t. The candidates who win in today’s market are not the ones who apply the most; they’re the ones who position themselves the best. They understand that every interaction—from résumé to interview—is a marketing moment that communicates value, confidence, and readiness. This shift requires moving from “application mode” to “value proposition mode.” It means seeing yourself not as a job seeker, but as a solution provider—someone who understands what organizations need and can articulate precisely how you meet that need. Confidence, resilience, and intentionality are no longer optional; they are competitive necessities. Those who master the mindset of strategy, storytelling, and self-worth don’t just search for jobs—they attract opportunities. This white paper explores how to rewire your job search approach for measurable results—transforming effort into traction and traction into offers.

    2
    Like Comment

    To view or add a comment, sign in

    258 followers

    • Report this post

    The job search has changed—dramatically. What once worked five years ago no longer guarantees results today. In this new landscape, success is not about how many applications you send—it’s about the strategy behind each one. The candidates who win in today’s market are not the ones who apply the most; they’re the ones who position themselves the best. Confidence, resilience, and intentionality are no longer optional; they are competitive necessities. Those who master the mindset of strategy, storytelling, and self-worth don’t just search for jobs—they attract opportunities. This white paper explores how to rewire your job search approach for measurable results—transforming effort into traction and traction into offers.
